description: Reference identifier for the evidence package (representment documentation) submitted by the merchant to contest the chargeback. Points to the document management system location containing delivery confirmations, signed receipts, authorization records, and other supporting evidence.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: The principal business event timestamp recording when the chargeback was formally filed by the issuing bank with the card scheme. This is the scheme-recognized filing date used to calculate all response deadlines and compliance windows per card scheme rules.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; ISO 8583; Regulation E (Federal Reserve)

description: Indicates whether this chargeback is classified as fraud-related based on the reason code category (True) or non-fraud (False). Fraud chargebacks (e.g., Visa reason code 10.x, Mastercard reason code 4837/4863) are tracked separately for fraud reporting, chargeback ratio monitoring, and Fraud Detection and Prevention Platform feedback loops.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS; FinCEN BSA

description: Indicates whether this chargeback is for a partial amount of the original transaction (True) or for the full transaction amount (False). Partial chargebacks occur when only a portion of the goods or services are disputed.

The party determined to bear financial liability for the chargeback upon final resolution. MERCHANT: merchant absorbs the loss; ISSUER: issuing bank absorbs the loss; ACQUIRER: acquiring bank absorbs the loss; SCHEME: card scheme absorbs the loss (rare, typically in compliance cases).
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Amex Chargeback Guidelines

description: The full amount of the original payment transaction that is the subject of this chargeback, in the original transaction currency. Used to identify partial chargebacks where chargeback_amount is less than the full transaction amount.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; ISO 8583

description: ISO 4217 three-letter currency code for the currency in which the original payment transaction was denominated. Enables currency reconciliation in cross-border dispute scenarios involving Foreign Exchange (FX) or Dynamic Currency Conversion (DCC).
reference: ISO 4217; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Indicates whether this chargeback has been flagged as contributing to the merchant's chargeback ratio threshold breach under card scheme monitoring programs (e.g., Visa Chargeback Monitoring Program - VCMP, Mastercard Excessive Chargeback Program - ECP). Merchants exceeding scheme thresholds face fines and potential termination.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS

description: Scheme-mandated deadline by which the merchant must submit a representment (rebuttal) package to challenge the chargeback. Distinct from response_deadline in that it specifically governs the representment submission window after the merchant elects to contest the chargeback.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: Actual timestamp when the merchant submitted the representment package to contest the chargeback. A representment is the merchant's formal response disputing the chargeback, including evidence such as delivery confirmation, signed receipts, or fraud documentation.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: Final resolution outcome of the chargeback after all dispute cycles are exhausted. MERCHANT_WON: representment was successful and chargeback reversed; ISSUER_WON: chargeback upheld and merchant debited; SPLIT_LIABILITY: liability shared between parties; WITHDRAWN: cardholder or issuer withdrew the dispute; EXPIRED: chargeback expired due to inaction.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Amex Chargeback Guidelines; Discover Network Rules

description: Timestamp when the chargeback reached its final resolution and the case was closed. Marks the end of the chargeback lifecycle. Used for SLA compliance measurement and financial reconciliation timing.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: Scheme-mandated deadline by which the acquiring bank or merchant must submit a representment or accept the chargeback. Calculated from the filing timestamp per card scheme rules (e.g., Visa typically allows 30 calendar days; Mastercard 45 calendar days). Missing this deadline results in automatic chargeback acceptance.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Amex Chargeback Guidelines; Discover Network Rules

description: Actual timestamp when the merchant or acquiring bank submitted their response (representment package or acceptance) to the chargeback. Compared against response_deadline to determine deadline compliance status.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: Card scheme-assigned case identifier for the chargeback as provided by Visa, Mastercard, Amex, or Discover through their dispute resolution portals (e.g., Visa Resolve Online - VROL, Mastercard MATCH). Used for scheme-level tracking and arbitration submissions.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Amex Chargeback Guidelines; Discover Network Rules

description: Fee charged by the card scheme (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover) for processing the chargeback. Scheme fees are assessed per chargeback filing and may vary by scheme, reason code, and merchant chargeback ratio tier. Debited from the acquiring bank and typically passed through to the merchant.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Amex Chargeback Guidelines; Discover Network Rules

description: Indicates whether the internal SLA for chargeback processing was breached (True) for this case. Internal SLAs govern the time from chargeback receipt to merchant notification, evidence collection, and representment submission, distinct from the scheme-mandated response deadlines.
reference: Dispute and Chargeback Management System; Internal SLA Policy

description: Name of the operational system of record from which this chargeback record was sourced (e.g., Dispute and Chargeback Management System). Supports data lineage tracking and reconciliation across the lakehouse architecture.
reference: Data Warehouse and Analytics Platform; SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act)

description: The date on which the original payment transaction that is the subject of this chargeback was processed. Used to verify chargeback filing timeliness against scheme-mandated windows (e.g., Visa allows chargebacks up to 120 days from transaction date in most cases).
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Regulation E (Federal Reserve)

description: Timestamp of the most recent update to the chargeback record in the system. Tracks the last modification across any field in the chargeback lifecycle, supporting audit trail requirements and change detection for downstream data consumers.
reference: SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act); GDPR Article 5; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: Externally-known, human-readable business identifier for the dispute case, assigned by the Dispute and Chargeback Management System at case creation. Used in all correspondence with cardholders, merchants, and card schemes. Format: DSP-{YYYY}-{8-digit sequence}.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Internal Dispute Operations Standard

description: Current operational status of the dispute case within the Dispute and Chargeback Management System. Distinct from lifecycle_stage — status reflects the internal workflow state (e.g., awaiting merchant response, under analyst review) while lifecycle_stage reflects the card scheme process stage.
reference: Internal Dispute Operations Standard; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Date on which the formal chargeback was filed with the card scheme by the issuing bank. Marks the start of the chargeback lifecycle stage and triggers merchant response deadlines. Null if the case has not yet reached chargeback stage.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; CFPB Regulation E

description: Card scheme-specific reason code assigned to the chargeback, identifying the precise basis for the dispute (e.g., Visa reason code 10.4 for Other Fraud — Card Absent, Mastercard reason code 4853 for Cardholder Dispute). Null for retrieval requests that have not yet escalated to chargeback. description: Date and time when the dispute case was formally closed and all activities concluded. Null if the case is still open. Used to calculate total case duration and measure resolution cycle time against SLA targets.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; CFPB Regulation E

description: Timestamp when this dispute case record was first created in the data platform. Audit trail field for data lineage and SOX compliance. May differ from open_timestamp if there is a lag between case opening in the source system and ingestion into the lakehouse.
reference: SOX Section 404; GDPR Article 5(2); Internal Data Governance Standard

description: The gross monetary amount under dispute, expressed in the transaction currency. Represents the full or partial amount of the original transaction being contested. Used for financial adjustment calculations and regulatory reporting.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; CFPB Regulation E; PCI DSS

description: Channel through which the cardholder or issuing bank initiated the dispute. Values: online (web portal), phone (call center), branch (in-person), mobile_app (mobile banking app), scheme_portal (direct card scheme submission). Used for channel analytics and process optimization.
reference: CFPB Regulation E; Internal Dispute Operations Standard; Visa Dispute Resolution Management

description: High-level classification of the dispute category as defined by card scheme rules. Values: fraud (unauthorized transaction), authorization (authorization-related issue), processing_error (duplicate, incorrect amount, etc.), consumer_dispute (goods/services not received or not as described), compliance (regulatory or scheme compliance violation).
reference: Visa Core Rules Dispute Resolution; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; ISO 8583

description: Indicates whether the merchant has submitted an evidence package (e.g., transaction receipts, delivery confirmation, signed authorization) in response to the chargeback. True = evidence package received. Used to track representment completeness and SLA compliance.
reference: Visa Core Rules Dispute Resolution; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; Internal Dispute Operations Standard

description: Interchange Reimbursement Fee amount associated with the disputed transaction, which may be reversed or adjusted as part of the chargeback process. Relevant for financial reconciliation between the acquiring bank and issuing bank.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Federal Reserve Regulation II (Durbin Amendment)

description: Indicates whether the original transaction was authenticated using 3-D Secure (3DS) protocol at the time of authorization. True = 3DS authentication was performed. Relevant for liability shift determination — 3DS-authenticated transactions shift chargeback liability to the issuer.
reference: EMVCo 3-D Secure Specification; Visa Secure (Verified by Visa); Mastercard Identity Check; PSD2 SCA Requirements

description: Indicates whether the dispute has been confirmed as a fraudulent transaction by the fraud investigation team or card scheme ruling. True = confirmed fraud; False = not confirmed as fraud. Used for fraud analytics, SAR filing decisions, and AML reporting.
reference: FinCEN BSA/SAR Filing Requirements; FATF Recommendation 20; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Bank Identification Number (BIN) / Issuer Identification Number (IIN) of the issuing bank that initiated the dispute on behalf of the cardholder. First 6-8 digits of the PAN identifying the card issuer. Used for dispute routing and interchange settlement.
reference: ISO 7812; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS

description: Timestamp of the most recent update to this dispute case record in the data platform. Used for incremental ETL processing, change detection, and audit trail compliance.
reference: SOX Section 404; GDPR Article 5(2); Internal Data Governance Standard

description: The party determined to bear financial liability for the disputed transaction based on card scheme rules, authentication status, and dispute outcome. Drives financial adjustment routing between issuing bank, acquiring bank, and merchant.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; EMVCo 3DS Liability Shift Rules; PSD2 Article 74

description: Current stage in the dispute lifecycle workflow as governed by card scheme rules. Progresses from retrieval_request through chargeback, representment, pre_arbitration, arbitration, to closed. Drives SLA deadlines and permissible actions at each stage.
reference: Visa Core Rules Dispute Resolution; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; Visa Dispute Resolution Management

description: Merchant Identification Number assigned by the acquiring bank to uniquely identify the merchant in card scheme networks. Denormalized from the merchant record for direct dispute reporting and card scheme submission without requiring a join.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS Requirement 12

description: Free-text field capturing analyst notes, investigation findings, key decisions, and case narrative throughout the dispute lifecycle. Supports audit trail requirements and knowledge transfer between analysts. Not intended for structured data — structured fields take precedence.
reference: SOX Section 404; Internal Dispute Operations Standard; GDPR Article 5(2)

description: Date and time when the dispute case was formally opened in the Dispute and Chargeback Management System. Serves as the principal business event timestamp and the anchor for all SLA deadline calculations per card scheme rules.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; CFPB Regulation E 12 CFR Part 1005

description: The full monetary amount of the original payment transaction that is the subject of this dispute, in the original transaction currency. Provides context for partial dispute scenarios where dispute_amount is less than the full transaction value.

description: ISO 4217 three-letter currency code of the original payment transaction. May differ from dispute_currency_code in Dynamic Currency Conversion (DCC) scenarios or cross-border transactions.

description: Calendar date on which the original payment transaction was processed. Used to calculate dispute filing timeliness against card scheme deadlines (e.g., Visa's 120-day chargeback window from transaction date).
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Chargeback Guide; CFPB Regulation E 12 CFR Part 1005

description: Operational priority assigned to the dispute case for analyst workqueue management. Critical cases (e.g., large-value disputes, regulatory escalations, VIP cardholders) receive expedited handling. Drives SLA tier assignment and escalation workflows.

description: Indicates whether this dispute case is subject to mandatory regulatory reporting obligations (e.g., CFPB complaint reporting, PSD2 incident reporting, OFAC screening result). True = case must be included in regulatory submissions.

description: Date on which the merchant submitted a representment (rebuttal) package challenging the chargeback. Null if no representment has been filed. Used to track merchant response timeliness and advance the lifecycle stage.

description: Final resolution outcome of the dispute case once closed. Indicates whether the dispute was resolved in favor of the cardholder (won), merchant (lost), mutually settled, withdrawn by the initiating party, written off, or partially recovered. Populated only when case_status = closed.

description: Net financial adjustment amount applied to the settlement account as a result of the dispute resolution. May differ from dispute_amount due to partial recoveries, fees, or write-offs. Negative value indicates a debit to the merchant; positive indicates a credit to the cardholder.

description: Unique case reference number assigned by the card scheme (Visa or Mastercard) for arbitration or compliance proceedings associated with this financial adjustment. Used for scheme-level dispute tracking and correspondence.
Fee assessed by the card scheme (Visa or Mastercard) for processing the dispute event, such as arbitration filing fees or compliance assessment charges. Included in the net settlement calculation.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI Security Standards Council

description: Net amount posted to the settlement batch after applying any scheme fees, interchange adjustments, or currency conversion. May differ from adjustment_amount when FX conversion or fee netting applies.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Transaction Processing Platform

description: ISO 4217 three-letter currency code for the settlement amount. May differ from adjustment_currency_code when Dynamic Currency Conversion (DCC) or cross-border settlement applies.
reference: ISO 4217; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Identifies the operational system of record that originated this financial adjustment record (e.g., Dispute and Chargeback Management System, ERP Oracle Financials, card scheme network feed, or manual entry). Supports data lineage and reconciliation.
reference: Data Warehouse and Analytics Platform; SOX

description: Timestamp of the most recent modification to this financial adjustment record. Supports change data capture (CDC) in the Databricks Silver Layer and audit compliance.
reference: SOX; GDPR Article 5; Data Warehouse and Analytics Platform

description: Primary key for ratio_monitor

Foreign key linking to settlement.acquirer. Business justification: Scheme chargeback ratio monitoring programs (Visa VDMP, Mastercard MATCH) track ratios at acquirer level. ratio_monitor has mid and mcc as text but no acquirer FK. Direct acquirer_id enables acquirer-

Foreign key linking to product.card_program. Business justification: Chargeback ratio thresholds and monitoring programs (VDMP, VCFP, EFM) are card program-specific with different enrollment criteria and fees. This link enables program-specific ratio threshold enforcem

Foreign key linking to merchant.merchant_account. Business justification: Scheme monitoring programs operate at MID/account level. A merchant can have multiple merchant_accounts with distinct MIDs. Linking ratio_monitor to merchant_account enables direct enforcement of enha

Foreign key linking to merchant.merchant. Business justification: Scheme monitoring programs (VDMP, ECP) track chargeback/fraud ratios per merchant. ratio_monitor has mid as denormalized text but no merchant_id FK. Risk and compliance teams must link monitoring reco

Foreign key linking to merchant.merchant_risk_profile. Business justification: When a ratio_monitor record shows threshold breach (VDMP/ECP enrollment), risk ops must update the merchant_risk_profile to adjust reserve_percentage, risk_tier, and enhanced_monitoring_required. This

Foreign key linking to dispute.rule_config. Business justification: ratio_monitor tracks merchant chargeback ratios against card scheme program thresholds. rule_config governs automated dispute handling decisions including ratio thresholds (standard_threshold_percenta
Foreign key linking to network.scheme. Business justification: Chargeback ratio monitoring is performed per scheme; scheme_id enables the Ratio Monitoring Report used for compliance.

Foreign key linking to network.scheme_parameter. Business justification: Scheme monitoring programs (Visa VDMP/VFMP, Mastercard MATCH) define chargeback and fraud ratio thresholds via scheme_parameter records. ratio_monitor must reference the governing scheme_parameter to
The date the acquiring bank notified the merchant of the chargeback ratio threshold breach and required remediation actions.
The monetary penalty or assessment fee imposed by the card scheme for the chargeback ratio threshold breach during this monitoring period. Fees escalate with severity and duration of non-compliance.
reference: Visa VDMP Fee Schedule, Mastercard MCP Fee Schedule

description: Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the assessment fee amount (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP).
The calculated chargeback ratio expressed as a percentage: (total_chargeback_count / total_transaction_count) * 100. Core metric for determining scheme program threshold breaches.
Current compliance status of the merchant with respect to card scheme chargeback ratio requirements for this monitoring period.
The number of consecutive months the merchant has been enrolled in the chargeback monitoring program as of this monitoring period. Used to track escalation and potential termination risk.
The date and time when this chargeback ratio monitoring record was first created in the system. Audit trail for record lifecycle tracking.
The excessive chargeback ratio threshold percentage defined by the card scheme program for this monitoring period. Typically 1.8% for Visa VDMP excessive threshold.
The subset of total chargebacks that were classified as fraud-related (reason codes 10.4, 4837, etc.) during the monitoring period. Used for Visa Fraud Monitoring Program (VFM) tracking.
reference: Visa VFM, Card Scheme Reason Codes

description: The calculated fraud chargeback ratio expressed as a percentage: (fraud_chargeback_count / total_transaction_count) * 100. Used for Visa Fraud Monitoring Program (VFM) compliance.
Boolean flag indicating whether the merchant is classified as high-risk by the acquirer or card scheme, which may result in stricter chargeback ratio thresholds or enhanced monitoring.
Four-digit code classifying the merchants business type. Certain MCCs have different chargeback ratio thresholds or heightened monitoring requirements.

The minimum number of chargebacks required during the monitoring period for the merchant to be subject to the chargeback monitoring program. Typically 100 chargebacks for Visa VDMP standard threshold.
The minimum number of transactions required during the monitoring period for the merchant to be subject to the chargeback monitoring program. Typically 1,000 transactions for Visa VDMP.
The calendar month for which this chargeback ratio is calculated and monitored. Typically the first day of the month (YYYY-MM-01 format).

The specific card scheme chargeback monitoring program under which this merchant is being tracked. Examples: VDMP (Visa Dispute Monitoring Program), VFM (Visa Fraud Monitoring), MCP (Mastercard Chargeback Program), ECMP (Excessive Chargeback Merchant Program).
reference: Visa VDMP Rules, Mastercard MCP Standards

description: Current lifecycle status of this monitoring record. Active indicates ongoing monitoring; Closed indicates the monitoring period has concluded; Under Review indicates pending scheme or acquirer review.
Free-text field for operational notes, comments, or context regarding this monitoring record, remediation efforts, or communications with the merchant or card scheme.
The date the merchant was enrolled into the card scheme chargeback monitoring program due to threshold breach. Null if merchant is not currently enrolled.
The date the merchant successfully exited the card scheme chargeback monitoring program by achieving compliance. Null if merchant is still enrolled or was never enrolled.
Three-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country or region code where the merchant operates. Card scheme monitoring programs may have regional variations in thresholds and rules.
Reference identifier or document number for the merchants chargeback remediation plan submitted to address the threshold breach. Required for merchants enrolled in monitoring programs.
The end date of the rolling review period used to calculate the chargeback ratio.
The start date of the rolling review period used to calculate the chargeback ratio. Card schemes typically use a rolling 12-month or 6-month window.
The date the card scheme officially notified the acquirer and merchant of the chargeback ratio threshold breach and program enrollment.
The standard chargeback ratio threshold percentage defined by the card scheme program for this monitoring period. Typically 0.9% for Visa VDMP standard threshold.

The severity level of the chargeback ratio threshold breach, if any. Indicates which tier of the card scheme monitoring program the merchant has entered. None indicates compliance; other values indicate escalating severity.
reference: Visa VDMP Thresholds, Mastercard MCP Thresholds

description: The total number of chargebacks received against the merchant during the monitoring period month. Numerator for chargeback ratio calculation.
The total number of transactions processed by the merchant during the monitoring period month. Denominator for chargeback ratio calculation.
The date and time when this chargeback ratio monitoring record was last modified. Audit trail for record lifecycle tracking.
Primary key for rule_config
Foreign key linking to network.scheme. Business justification: Dispute rule configurations are defined per card scheme; scheme_id is required for the Rule Engine to select appropriate rules.

Foreign key linking to network.scheme_parameter. Business justification: Dispute automation rules (auto-accept, auto-reject, evidence requirements) are directly derived from scheme parameters such as reason code eligibility, time limits, and liability rules. Linking rule_c
Configuration parameters or metadata required to execute the auto action, stored as JSON or key-value pairs.
Approval workflow status indicating whether the rule configuration has been reviewed and authorized for production use.
Identifier or name of the user or role who approved this rule configuration for production deployment.
Date and time when this rule configuration was approved, recorded in ISO 8601 format.
Automated action to be taken when the rule conditions are met during dispute processing.
Free-text field capturing compliance considerations, regulatory alignment, or audit notes related to this rule configuration.
Structured or semi-structured expression defining the logical conditions under which this rule is triggered. May be JSON, XML, or domain-specific language.
Date and time when this dispute rule configuration record was first created in the system, recorded in ISO 8601 format.
Maximum age of the dispute in days for this rule to apply, enabling time-sensitive rule logic.
Date from which this dispute rule configuration becomes active and applicable to dispute processing.
Indicates whether supporting evidence must be present for the rule action to be executed.
Comma-separated or pipe-separated list of evidence types required for this rule to trigger, such as delivery_confirmation, signature, invoice.
Cumulative count of how many times this rule has been executed since activation, used for monitoring and analytics.
Date on which this dispute rule configuration ceases to be active. Null indicates no expiration.
Cumulative count of failed rule executions where the rule action could not be completed due to errors or exceptions.
Boolean flag indicating whether this rule configuration is currently active and being applied in dispute processing workflows.
Date and time when this rule was last executed in a dispute processing workflow, recorded in ISO 8601 format.
Maximum number of disputes this rule can process within a defined time window, used for rate limiting or risk control.
Merchant classification or segment to which this rule applies, enabling segment-specific dispute handling strategies. May reference MCC (Merchant Category Code) ranges or internal merchant tiers.
Specific Merchant Identification Number to which this rule applies. Null indicates the rule applies broadly per merchant segment or globally.
Identifier or name of the user or system process that last modified this rule configuration record.
Date and time when this dispute rule configuration record was last modified, recorded in ISO 8601 format.
Comma-separated list of email addresses or user identifiers to be notified when this rule is triggered.
Indicates whether a notification must be sent to stakeholders when this rule is triggered.
Numeric priority ranking for rule evaluation order when multiple rules may apply to the same dispute. Lower numbers indicate higher priority.
Chargeback or dispute reason code to which this rule applies. May be a specific code or a wildcard pattern for multiple codes. Aligns with card scheme reason code taxonomies.
Unique business identifier code for the dispute rule configuration, used for external reference and system integration.
Detailed textual description of the rule logic, business rationale, and intended use case for this dispute rule configuration.
Human-readable name of the dispute rule configuration describing its purpose and scope.
Business owner or team responsible for maintaining and governing this dispute rule configuration.
Current lifecycle status of the dispute rule configuration indicating whether it is actively applied in dispute processing.
Classification of the dispute rule configuration indicating the type of automated action or decision logic it governs.
Cumulative count of successful rule executions where the rule conditions were met and the action was successfully applied.
Monetary threshold value that triggers or qualifies the rule action. Used for auto-accept or auto-representment eligibility based on dispute amount.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the threshold amount.
Time window in hours for evaluating max dispute count or other time-bound rule conditions.
Maximum age of the original transaction in days for this rule to apply, used for representment eligibility assessment.
Version number of this rule configuration, incremented with each modification to support audit trail and rollback capabilities.
Minimum historical win rate percentage for similar disputes required for auto-representment eligibility under this rule.
Identifier or name of the user or system process that created this rule configuration record.

business_glossary_term: Bank Identification Number (BIN) Interchange Rule ID
description: Unique surrogate primary key identifying a BIN-level interchange qualification rule record in the silver layer lakehouse. Entity role: MASTER_RESOURCE — represents a configuration/rule resource governing interchange qualification for a BIN range.

Foreign key linking to product.card_program. Business justification: BIN-based interchange rules determine rates for specific card programs (rewards, corporate, prepaid). Issuers configure interchange eligibility by card program. Critical for interchange qualification

Foreign key linking to fx.currency_pair. Business justification: BIN-level interchange rules define eligible currency pairs for cross-border routing and rate application. Required for BIN-based currency eligibility validation and cross-border transaction routing de
Reference to the issuing bank or financial institution that issued the card associated with this BIN range. The issuing bank is the cardholder's bank in the payment chain.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Federal Reserve Regulation II

description: Foreign key linking to interchange.irf_rate_category. Business justification: bin_interchange_rule references IRF category; adds irf_rate_category_id FK

Foreign key linking to interchange.program. Business justification: bin_interchange_rule.scheme_program_code (STRING) stores the card scheme program code that governs the BIN-level interchange qualification rule (e.g., Visa CPS, Mastercard Merit). The program product

Foreign key linking to risk.risk_profile. Business justification: BIN-level interchange rules are risk-segmented by issuer. Issuer risk profiles determine rule applicability, rate eligibility, and Durbin regulation status. Critical for issuer onboarding and rate tab
Reference to the card scheme (payment network brand) governing this interchange rule, such as Visa, Mastercard, American Express, or Discover. Links to the scheme reference product.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; EMVCo

description: Reference to the bin_interchange_rule_id of the newer rule that supersedes this record when rule_status is 'superseded'. Enables rule lineage tracking and historical interchange qualification reconstruction.
reference: SOX; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Specifies the type of authorization associated with this interchange rule: pre-authorization (hotel/car rental), final authorization, or incremental authorization. Different authorization types may qualify for distinct interchange rates.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; ISO 8583

description: Indicates whether Address Verification System (AVS) check is required for a transaction to qualify for this interchange rate category. AVS validation is a fraud prevention measure commonly required for card-not-present interchange qualification.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS v4.0

description: The ending BIN (Bank Identification Number) of the BIN range to which this interchange qualification rule applies. Together with bin_range_start, defines the full BIN range covered by this rule.
reference: ISO/IEC 7812; PCI DSS v4.0; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: The starting BIN (Bank Identification Number), also known as IIN (Issuer Identification Number), of the BIN range to which this interchange qualification rule applies. Supports 6-digit legacy BINs and 8-digit extended BINs per ISO/IEC 7812.
reference: ISO/IEC 7812; PCI DSS v4.0; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Indicates whether this interchange rule applies to card-not-present (CNP) transactions, such as e-commerce, mail order, or telephone order (MOTO) transactions. CNP transactions typically carry higher interchange rates due to elevated fraud risk.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS v4.0

description: Indicates whether this interchange rule applies to card-present (CP) transactions, where the physical card or device is used at a POS or mPOS terminal. Card-present transactions typically qualify for lower interchange rates than card-not-present.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; EMVCo EMV Chip Specifications

description: Indicates whether this interchange rule applies to contactless NFC (Near Field Communication) transactions, including tap-to-pay and mobile wallet payments. Contactless qualification may carry distinct interchange rates from contact chip transactions.
reference: EMVCo Contactless Specifications; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; NFC Forum

description: ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code of the issuing country associated with this BIN range (e.g., USA, GBR, DEU). Determines domestic vs. cross-border interchange rate applicability and regulatory jurisdiction.
reference: ISO 3166-1; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; GDPR; PSD2

description: Timestamp when this BIN interchange rule record was first created in the data platform. Serves as the RECORD_AUDIT_CREATED field for audit trail and data lineage purposes. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
reference: SOX; GDPR Article 5; PCI DSS v4.0

description: Indicates whether this interchange rule applies specifically to cross-border transactions where the issuing country differs from the acquiring country. Cross-border transactions typically attract higher interchange rates and additional scheme fees.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PSD2; SWIFT

description: Indicates the level of transaction data (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3) required for interchange qualification under this rule. Level 2/3 data (enhanced transaction detail for B2B/commercial cards) may qualify for lower interchange rates.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS v4.0

description: Reason code explaining why a BIN range is exempt from Durbin Amendment regulated interchange caps. Applicable only when durbin_regulated_flag is False. Small issuers (assets < $10B), government-administered programs, and certain prepaid products qualify for exemption.
reference: Federal Reserve Regulation II (Durbin Amendment); Dodd-Frank Act Section 1075; CFPB

description: Indicates whether this BIN range is subject to the Durbin Amendment interchange fee caps under Federal Reserve Regulation II. True = regulated (issuer assets ≥ $10B threshold, capped at 21 cents + 0.05% + 1 cent fraud adjustment); False = unregulated (exempt issuers, higher interchange applies).
reference: Federal Reserve Regulation II (Durbin Amendment); Dodd-Frank Act Section 1075

description: The date on which this BIN interchange qualification rule ceases to be effective. Null indicates an open-ended rule with no defined expiry. Used to manage rule versioning and historical interchange cost analysis. Serves as EFFECTIVE_UNTIL for this MASTER_RESOURCE entity.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Federal Reserve Regulation II

description: The date from which this BIN interchange qualification rule becomes effective and is applied during transaction processing and interchange qualification. Aligns with scheme fee schedule publication cycles (typically semi-annual for Visa/Mastercard). Serves as EFFECTIVE_FROM for this MASTER_RESOURCE entity.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Federal Reserve Regulation II

description: Indicates whether EMV (Europay Mastercard Visa) chip processing is required for a transaction to qualify for this interchange rate category. EMV chip transactions reduce fraud liability and may qualify for preferential interchange rates.
reference: EMVCo EMV Chip Specifications; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Indicates whether this interchange rule applies to installment payment transactions, including BNPL (Buy Now Pay Later) arrangements. Installment transactions may carry distinct interchange qualification criteria.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; CFPB

description: ISO 4217 three-letter currency code in which the interchange fixed fee is denominated (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP). Required for cross-border interchange cost calculations and multi-currency acquiring portfolios.
reference: ISO 4217; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: The fixed per-transaction fee component of the interchange, expressed in the applicable currency (e.g., 0.1000 = $0.10 per transaction). Combined with interchange_rate_percent to compute total interchange cost per transaction.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Federal Reserve Regulation II

description: The percentage-based component of the interchange fee applied to the transaction amount for qualifying transactions under this BIN rule (e.g., 1.6500 represents 1.65%). Used in cost-of-acceptance calculations for acquiring portfolios. Serves as the MEASUREMENT_OR_VALUE for this MASTER_RESOURCE entity.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Federal Reserve Regulation II; SOX

description: Comma-separated list of MCC (Merchant Category Code) values that are included or excluded from this interchange rule, as determined by mcc_restriction_type. For example, '5411,5412,5499' for grocery MCCs. Null when mcc_restriction_type is 'all'.
reference: ISO 18245 (MCC Standard); Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Defines how MCC (Merchant Category Code) restrictions apply to this interchange rule: 'all' means no MCC restriction (applies to all MCCs), 'include' means only specified MCCs qualify, 'exclude' means specified MCCs are excluded, 'none' means no transactions qualify regardless of MCC.
reference: ISO 18245 (MCC Standard); Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Indicates whether this interchange rule applies to recurring or subscription-based transactions. Recurring transactions may qualify for specific interchange programs with distinct rate structures.

Card scheme-defined geographic region code associated with this BIN range (e.g., 'AP' for Asia Pacific, 'CEMEA' for Central Europe Middle East Africa, 'LAC' for Latin America and Caribbean, 'NA' for North America). Used for cross-border interchange rate determination.

Externally-known unique business code identifying this BIN interchange qualification rule, used in downstream transaction processing and interchange qualification engines. Serves as the BUSINESS_IDENTIFIER for this MASTER_RESOURCE entity.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; ISO 8583

description: Free-text description providing additional context about this BIN interchange qualification rule, including any special conditions, exceptions, or notes from the card scheme or compliance team.
Human-readable descriptive name for the BIN interchange qualification rule (e.g., 'Visa Debit Regulated CPS Retail'). Serves as the IDENTITY_LABEL for this MASTER_RESOURCE entity.

Current lifecycle status of this BIN interchange qualification rule. Active rules are applied in real-time interchange qualification; superseded rules have been replaced by newer versions; expired rules are past their effective end date. Serves as the LIFECYCLE_STATUS for this MASTER_RESOURCE entity.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; SOX

description: The native rule identifier or reference code from the originating operational system (Transaction Processing Platform or Payment Gateway) from which this BIN interchange rule was sourced. Supports data lineage and reconciliation back to the system of record.

Indicates whether 3-D Secure (3DS) authentication is required for card-not-present transactions to qualify for this interchange rate. 3DS-authenticated transactions may qualify for lower interchange rates and shift fraud liability to the issuer. Relevant under PSD2 SCA requirements.
reference: EMVCo 3-D Secure Specification; PSD2 Article 97 (SCA); Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Indicates whether transactions using a DPAN (Device Primary Account Number) or network token issued by a TSP (Token Service Provider) for this BIN range are eligible for this interchange rate. Tokenized transactions may qualify for distinct interchange rates.
reference: EMVCo Tokenization Framework; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PCI DSS v4.0

description: Maximum transaction amount (in the fee currency) eligible for this interchange rate category. Transactions exceeding this threshold may be subject to different interchange rates or require additional qualification criteria.

Minimum transaction amount (in the fee currency) required for a transaction to qualify for this interchange rate category. Transactions below this threshold may downgrade to a less favorable interchange tier.

Timestamp when this BIN interchange rule record was last modified in the data platform. Supports change tracking, rule versioning audit, and regulatory compliance reporting. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
reference: SOX; GDPR Article 5; PCI DSS v4.0

description: Sequential version number of this BIN interchange rule record, incremented each time the rule is updated. Supports rule versioning and historical interchange qualification audit trails required for regulatory compliance and dispute resolution.
reference: SOX; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Unique identifier for the cross-border fee rule record. Primary key for this entity.

Foreign key linking to fx.currency_pair. Business justification: Cross-border fee rules explicitly define fees per currency pair corridor (e.g., USD→EUR vs EUR→GBP). Core business requirement for currency-pair-specific cross-border fee calculation and routing.

Foreign key linking to fx.payment_corridor. Business justification: Cross-border fee rules map to payment corridors defining country-currency-rail combinations. Essential for corridor-based fee routing, regulatory compliance, and settlement path determination in cross

Foreign key linking to product.payment_product. Business justification: Cross-border fee rules apply differently to payment products (international cards, multi-currency wallets). Payment products have varying cross-border eligibility. Required for international transacti

Foreign key linking to risk.risk_policy. Business justification: Cross-border fee rules enforce risk policies. High-risk corridors, sanctioned countries, and AML-flagged regions have elevated fees per risk policy. Required for regulatory compliance and fee rule con

Foreign key linking to interchange.program. Business justification: cross_border_fee_rule.scheme_program_code (STRING) stores the card scheme program code associated with the cross-border fee rule (e.g., a specific Visa or Mastercard cross-border program). The program
Reference to the card scheme (Visa, Mastercard, etc.) to which this cross-border fee rule applies.

business_glossary_term: Superseded By Cross-Border Fee Rule Identifier (ID)
description: Reference to the cross-border fee rule that replaces this rule when it is superseded. Supports rule lineage and historical analysis.
Indicates whether this cross-border fee rule applies to card-present transactions (physical POS) or card-not-present transactions (e-commerce, MOTO).
Timestamp when this cross-border fee rule record was first created in the system. Part of the audit trail for data lineage and compliance.
reference: Internal Data Governance Standard; SOX Compliance

description: Percentage-based cross-border assessment fee applied to the transaction amount when the issuing country differs from the acquiring country. Expressed as a decimal percentage (e.g., 1.25000 for 1.25%).
Fixed monetary fee applied per cross-border transaction, independent of transaction amount. Used in conjunction with or as an alternative to percentage-based fees.

business_glossary_term: Currency Conversion Fee Rate Percentage (Foreign Exchange - FX)
description: Percentage-based fee applied when currency conversion occurs in a cross-border transaction. Represents the FX markup over the wholesale exchange rate.
reference: ISO 20022 Financial Messaging; Internal FX Policy

business_glossary_term: Dynamic Currency Conversion (DCC) Fee Rate Percentage
description: Fee rate applied when Dynamic Currency Conversion is offered at the point of sale, allowing the cardholder to see the transaction amount in their home currency.
ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code representing the destination country (typically the issuing country or cardholder country of residence).
Regional grouping code for the destination country used for regional cross-border fee structures and compliance segmentation.
Date after which this cross-border fee rule is no longer active. Null indicates an open-ended rule with no expiration.
Date from which this cross-border fee rule becomes active and applicable to qualifying transactions. Part of the temporal validity window.
ISO 4217 three-letter currency code in which the cross-border fees are denominated and assessed.
Comma-separated list of four-digit MCC codes to which this cross-border fee rule applies or excludes, based on the MCC restriction type.
Defines how the MCC list is applied: all (applies to all MCCs), include (applies only to listed MCCs), exclude (applies to all except listed MCCs).
Free-text field for additional business context, special conditions, or operational notes related to this cross-border fee rule configuration.
ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code representing the country where the transaction originates (typically the acquiring country or merchant location country).
Regional grouping code for the originating country (e.g., EEA, NAFTA, APAC) used for regional cross-border fee structures.
Numeric priority ranking used to resolve conflicts when multiple cross-border fee rules match a transaction. Lower numbers indicate higher priority.
Indicates whether this cross-border fee rule is subject to specific regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., PSD2 in EU, Durbin Amendment caps).
Name of the regulatory framework or jurisdiction-specific regulation governing this cross-border fee rule (e.g., PSD2, Regulation II, local central bank rules).
reference: PSD2; Federal Reserve Regulation II; ECB Payment System Oversight

description: Business-readable unique code identifying this cross-border fee rule configuration. Used for operational reference and configuration management.
Descriptive name of the cross-border fee rule for business user identification and reporting purposes.
Current lifecycle status of the cross-border fee rule. Controls whether the rule is applied in production fee calculation processes.
Reference number or identifier of the official card scheme bulletin or publication that announced or mandated this cross-border fee rule.
Fixed fee component for SWIFT messaging and correspondent banking services used in cross-border settlement. Applies when international wire transfers are required for settlement.
Maximum transaction amount threshold for this cross-border fee rule to apply. Transactions above this amount may be subject to different fee structures or caps.
Minimum transaction amount threshold for this cross-border fee rule to apply. Transactions below this amount may be subject to different fee structures.
Type of payment transaction to which this cross-border fee rule applies. Different transaction types may have different cross-border fee structures.
User identifier or system account that last modified this cross-border fee rule record. Supports change management and audit requirements.
reference: Internal Data Governance Standard; SOX Compliance

description: Timestamp when this cross-border fee rule record was last modified. Supports change tracking and audit requirements.
reference: Internal Data Governance Standard; SOX Compliance

description: Version number of this cross-border fee rule configuration. Incremented with each modification to support change tracking and audit trails.
User identifier or system account that created this cross-border fee rule record. Supports audit trail and accountability requirements.
reference: Internal Data Governance Standard; SOX Compliance

business_glossary_term: Interchange Reimbursement Fee (IRF) Rate Category ID
description: Unique identifier for the interchange rate category. Primary key.
Reference to the card scheme (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover) that defines this interchange rate category.
The maximum number of hours between authorization and capture for transactions to qualify for this interchange rate category.
Indicates whether Address Verification System (AVS) validation is required to qualify for this interchange rate category.
The ending Bank Identification Number (BIN) or Issuer Identification Number (IIN) in the range of card products that qualify for this interchange rate category.
The starting Bank Identification Number (BIN) or Issuer Identification Number (IIN) in the range of card products that qualify for this interchange rate category.
Indicates whether this interchange rate category applies to card-not-present (CNP) transactions such as e-commerce, mail order, or telephone order.
Indicates whether this interchange rate category applies to card-present (CP) transactions where the physical card is presented at the Point of Sale (POS).
The unique code assigned by the card scheme to identify this interchange rate category (e.g., CPS_RETAIL, MERIT_III, OPTBLUE_TIER1).
The full business name of the interchange rate category as published by the card scheme (e.g., Visa CPS/Retail, Mastercard Merit III, Amex OptBlue Standard).
reference: Visa Product and Service Rules, Mastercard Standards

description: The classification of the interchange rate category based on card product type and transaction characteristics. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: standard|premium|commercial|debit|credit|prepaid|rewards|government|healthcare — 9 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Indicates whether contactless NFC transactions are eligible for this interchange rate category.
The timestamp when this interchange rate category record was first created in the system.
Indicates whether this interchange rate category applies to cross-border transactions where the acquiring and issuing banks are in different countries.

business_glossary_term: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 4217 Currency Code
description: The three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the transaction amount thresholds and interchange rates associated with this category.
Indicates whether Card Verification Value (CVV) validation is required to qualify for this interchange rate category.
Indicates whether this interchange rate category applies to domestic transactions where the acquiring and issuing banks are in the same country.
The interchange rate category code to which a transaction will be downgraded if it fails to meet the qualification criteria for this category.
A detailed textual description of the conditions under which a transaction will be downgraded from this interchange rate category to a lower-tier category.
Indicates whether this interchange rate category is subject to Durbin Amendment (Regulation II) caps for debit card transactions issued by regulated financial institutions.
The date on which this interchange rate category becomes active and applicable to transactions.
Indicates whether EMV chip authentication is required for transactions to qualify for this interchange rate category.
The date on which this interchange rate category ceases to be active and is no longer applicable to new transactions.
The current lifecycle status of this interchange rate category within the card schemes published rate structure.
The ending Merchant Category Code (MCC) in the range of merchant types that qualify for this interchange rate category.
The starting Merchant Category Code (MCC) in the range of merchant types that qualify for this interchange rate category.
Free-form internal notes and comments about this interchange rate category for operational reference and business context.
The code of the parent or umbrella interchange rate category under which this category is nested in the schemes taxonomy.
The priority ranking used to determine which interchange rate category applies when a transaction qualifies for multiple categories. Lower numbers indicate higher priority.
A detailed textual description of all the business and technical criteria that a transaction must meet to qualify for this interchange rate category.
reference: Visa Product and Service Rules, Mastercard Standards

description: Fee charged per ACH (bank transfer) transaction processed for the merchant, expressed in the plan's base currency. Typically lower than card transaction fees.
reference: NACHA ACH Network Rules; Payment Processing Platform

description: Separate MDR percentage applied specifically to American Express card transactions, which often have different pricing than Visa/Mastercard, expressed as a decimal.
reference: American Express Merchant Agreement; Card Scheme Rules

description: Fee charged per transaction when Address Verification System (AVS) checks are performed, expressed in the plan's base currency. Helps reduce fraud risk for card-not-present transactions.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Fraud Detection Platform

description: Fee charged per settlement batch submitted by the merchant, expressed in the plan's base currency. Applies to merchants using batch settlement rather than real-time capture.
reference: Transaction Processing Platform; Merchant Service Agreement

description: Fee charged to the merchant for each chargeback dispute, regardless of outcome, expressed in the plan's base currency. Covers administrative and processing costs.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: The minimum contract duration in months that the merchant must commit to when assigned this pricing plan. Null for month-to-month plans.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Contract Management Standards

description: Identifier of the system user or administrator who created this pricing plan record. Used for audit trail and accountability.
reference: Internal Data Management Standard; SOX Compliance

description: The date and time when this pricing plan record was first created in the system, in ISO 8601 format (yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX).
Markup percentage applied to the foreign exchange (FX) rate when Dynamic Currency Conversion (DCC) is offered to cardholders, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.0300 for 3.00% above spot rate).
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; FX and DCC Standards

description: MDR percentage applied specifically to debit card transactions, typically lower than credit card rates due to Regulation II (Durbin Amendment) caps, expressed as a decimal.
reference: Federal Reserve Regulation II; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: Penalty fee charged if the merchant terminates the agreement before the contract term expires, expressed in the plan's base currency. Null if no early termination penalty applies.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Contract Management Standards

description: The date from which this pricing plan becomes binding and applicable to assigned merchants. Transactions on or after this date are governed by this plan's terms.
The date on which this pricing plan ceases to be valid. Nullable for open-ended plans. Transactions after this date require a new plan assignment.
The markup percentage added on top of the actual interchange cost in interchange-plus pricing models (e.g., 0.0050 for 0.50% above interchange). Null for non-interchange-plus models.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Merchant Service Agreement

description: Indicates whether actual interchange fees (IRF) are passed through to the merchant at cost (true) or absorbed into a blended rate (false). True for interchange-plus and cost-plus models.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Interchange Reimbursement Fee Standards

description: Additional percentage fee applied to cross-border transactions where the card issuer is in a different country than the merchant, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.0100 for 1.00%).
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Cross-Border Payment Standards

description: The base percentage rate charged to the merchant per transaction, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.0275 for 2.75%). This is the primary revenue component for flat-rate and blended pricing models.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Merchant Service Agreement

description: Current lifecycle status of the pricing plan. Active plans are available for assignment; inactive plans are no longer offered but may still govern existing merchants; pending plans are under approval; suspended plans are temporarily unavailable; archived plans are historical records only.
reference: Merchant Management System - Pricing Configuration Module

description: The middle MDR percentage tier in tiered pricing models, applied to transactions that partially meet qualification criteria (e.g., keyed-in transactions, rewards cards), expressed as a decimal.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Tiered Pricing Standards

description: Fixed monthly account maintenance or service fee charged to the merchant, independent of transaction volume, expressed in the plan's base currency.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Payment Processing Platform

description: The minimum monthly fee the merchant must pay regardless of transaction volume, expressed in the plan's base currency. If monthly processing fees fall below this threshold, the merchant is charged the difference.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Payment Processing Platform

description: The highest MDR percentage tier in tiered pricing models, applied to transactions that fail qualification criteria (e.g., corporate cards, international cards, manually entered), expressed as a decimal.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Tiered Pricing Standards

description: Indicates whether this pricing plan is available for assignment to sub-merchants under a Payment Facilitator (PayFac) model. True if eligible for PayFac sub-merchants; false if only for direct merchants.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; PayFac Program Standards

description: Annual or monthly fee charged to merchants for PCI DSS compliance program administration, security scanning, and attestation support, expressed in the plan's base currency.
reference: PCI Security Standards Council; PCI DSS Requirements

description: Monthly penalty fee charged to merchants who fail to maintain PCI DSS compliance, expressed in the plan's base currency. Escalates with duration of non-compliance.
reference: PCI Security Standards Council; PCI DSS Requirements

description: Fixed fee charged per transaction in addition to the percentage-based MDR, expressed in the plan's base currency (e.g., 0.10 for $0.10 per transaction).
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Payment Processing Platform

description: Externally-known unique business identifier for the pricing plan, used in contracts and merchant communications.
reference: Merchant Management System - Pricing Configuration Module

description: Detailed description of the pricing plan, including key features, target merchant segments, and value proposition.
reference: Merchant Management System - Pricing Configuration Module

description: Human-readable name of the pricing plan (e.g., 'Standard Retail', 'Enterprise Plus', 'Startup Flat Rate').
reference: Merchant Management System - Pricing Configuration Module

description: The fundamental pricing structure applied to merchant transactions. Flat-rate charges a single percentage; interchange-plus passes through interchange costs plus a markup; tiered groups transactions into qualified/mid-qualified/non-qualified buckets; blended averages all transaction types; cost-plus adds a fixed margin to actual costs; custom indicates bespoke negotiated terms.
reference: PCI Security Standards Council; Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules

description: The lowest MDR percentage tier in tiered pricing models, applied to transactions that meet all qualification criteria (e.g., swiped consumer credit cards), expressed as a decimal.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Tiered Pricing Standards

description: Fee charged to the merchant for processing a refund or credit transaction, expressed in the plan's base currency. Some plans waive this fee; others charge per refund.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Transaction Processing Platform

description: Fee charged to the merchant for each retrieval request (pre-chargeback inquiry) from the issuing bank, expressed in the plan's base currency.
reference: Visa Core Rules; Mastercard Rules; Dispute and Chargeback Management System

description: One-time fee charged when a merchant is onboarded and assigned to this pricing plan, covering account setup, KYC processing, and initial configuration, expressed in the plan's base currency.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Merchant Management System

description: Fee charged for generating and delivering monthly merchant statements (paper or electronic), expressed in the plan's base currency.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Payment Processing Platform

description: Identifier of the system user or administrator who last modified this pricing plan record. Used for audit trail and accountability.
reference: Internal Data Management Standard; SOX Compliance

description: The date and time when this pricing plan record was last modified, in ISO 8601 format (yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX). Used for audit trail and change tracking.
Fee charged when a merchant obtains authorization via phone call to the authorization center rather than electronic authorization, expressed in the plan's base currency.
reference: Payment Gateway and Authorization Engine; Merchant Service Agreement

description: Monthly transaction volume threshold (in base currency) that triggers preferential pricing or tier upgrades. Used in volume-based pricing models where rates decrease as volume increases.
reference: Merchant Service Agreement; Payment Processing Platform

description: The government-issued tax identification number for the merchant business entity. Format varies by jurisdiction (e.g., EIN in USA, VAT number in EU, GST number in India). Used for tax reporting, regulatory compliance, and identity verification.
